# Role of BRCA1 in Triple-Negative Breast Cancer Cell Viability

## Abstract

Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) remains a significant clinical challenge. This study investigates the role of BRCA1 in regulating cell viability in MDA-MB-231 cells.

## Introduction

Breast cancer is the most common malignancy in women worldwide. BRCA1 is a well-known tumor suppressor gene involved in DNA repair. Previous studies have shown that BRCA1 mutations are frequently observed in TNBC. Understanding the functional role of BRCA1 in TNBC may lead to improved therapeutic strategies.

## Methods

### Cell Culture

MDA-MB-231 cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS. HeLa cells were used as a control cell line. Cells were maintained at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2.

### siRNA Transfection

BRCA1 knockdown was performed using siRNA transfection with Lipofectamine 3000. Scrambled siRNA was used as a negative control. Transfection efficiency was confirmed by Western blot analysis.

### Cell Viability Assay

Cell viability was measured using the MTT assay at 24, 48, and 72 hours post-transfection. Absorbance was read at 570 nm using a microplate reader.

## Results

BRCA1 knockdown significantly reduced cell viability in MDA-MB-231 cells (p < 0.01). The reduction was most pronounced at 72 hours post-transfection, with a 45% decrease compared to control. Western blot analysis confirmed that BRCA1 protein levels were reduced by approximately 80% following siRNA treatment. BRCA1 depletion also increased apoptosis markers, including cleaved PARP and caspase-3 activation.

## Discussion

Our findings demonstrate that BRCA1 plays a critical role in maintaining cell viability in TNBC cells. This is consistent with previous reports showing BRCA1 involvement in DNA damage response pathways. The observed increase in apoptosis suggests that BRCA1 loss sensitizes TNBC cells to programmed cell death. However, these results should be interpreted with caution as they are limited to a single cell line. Olaparib treatment may provide additional therapeutic benefit in BRCA1-deficient tumors. Future studies should examine the effect of BRCA1 knockdown in additional TNBC cell lines and in vivo models.
